refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall update the worksite model to reflect current equipment state, environmental conditions, and operational events with a p95 latency not exceeding the customer-defined target (baseline 1.0 s) across all supported update scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-007 requires explicit binding to measurable latency acceptance criteria and clarification of what constitutes 'worksite model' (equipment state, environment, events). The refinement anchors the requirement to the p95 latency constraint and acknowledges customer-defined variability in target thresholds. High safety criticality demands that latency directly impacts hazard detection and operator response time.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency from sensor acquisition through model update persistence across representative scenarios (equipment movement, obstacle detection, tele-operator command feedback). Validate p95 percentile against customer acceptance threshold. Correlate latency with safety-critical event detection (e.g., proximity alerts, stability warnings).
sub_frs: FR-007.1: Acquire real-time telemetry from vehicle sensors and subsystems with minimal acquisition latency.
FR-007.2: Transmit telemetry data to worksite model service with bounded network latency.
FR-007.3: Process and fuse telemetry into worksite model state representation.
FR-007.4: Persist updated worksite model state to accessible storage.
FR-007.5: Distribute updated worksite model to authorized consumers (operators, tele-operators, safety services) within latency budget.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized worksite model service with synchronous request-response telemetry ingestion and push-based model distribution.
DP-B: Distributed event-driven architecture with asynchronous telemetry streaming, local model caches, and eventual consistency model updates.

DP-C: Hybrid approach: synchronous telemetry ingestion for safety-critical events, asynchronous for non-critical state updates, with tiered model distribution (real-time for safety, eventual for operational awareness).
DP-D: Edge-local model caches on vehicle with periodic synchronization to central worksite model, reducing network round-trip latency.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid approach with safety-critical synchronous path and non-critical asynchronous path, tiered distribution, and explicit latency budgeting per update category.

potential_sources: Coupling between safety-critical telemetry path and non-critical path may introduce priority inversion if not carefully scheduled.
Coupling between model update frequency and network bandwidth; high-frequency updates may saturate links.
Coupling between tele-operator command latency and worksite model update latency; operator feedback loop depends on both.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C decouples safety-critical and operational latency requirements, reducing coupling. Explicit latency budgeting and path separation mitigate priority inversion risk. Requires clear definition of which telemetry is safety-critical vs. operational.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer can define which telemetry updates are safety-critical vs. operational. Risk: Ambiguity in classification may lead to incorrect path assignment.
Assumption: Network infrastructure supports differentiated service levels (QoS). Risk: If network is best-effort, safety-critical path may not meet latency target.
Assumption: Vehicle sensors can acquire and transmit telemetry at sufficient frequency to support p95 ≤ 1.0 s model update. Risk: Sensor acquisition latency or transmission delays may exceed budget.
Assumption: Worksite model service can process and persist updates within allocated latency budget. Risk: Database write latency or model fusion complexity may exceed budget.
Risk: Eventual consistency in non-critical path may cause operator confusion if model state diverges significantly from reality.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly define latency budgets for each sub-path (safety-critical: ≤ 200 ms, operational: ≤ 1.0 s), specify QoS requirements for network, and define model consistency guarantees (e.g., safety-critical updates are strongly consistent, operational updates are eventually consistent within 2 s).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: End-to-end worksite model update pipeline, including telemetry acquisition from Vehicle, transmission via Network Link, processing and persistence in central worksite model service, and distribution to consumers via Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces. Encompasses coordination of safety-critical and operational update paths.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingress: Real-time sensor telemetry from Vehicle (equipment state, position, orientation, sensor readings). Egress: Updated worksite model state to operators, tele-operators, and safety services. Latency-critical for both directions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety-critical notifications derived from worksite model updates (proximity alerts, stability warnings, hazard detections). Must meet p95 ≤ 200 ms latency for safety-critical events.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network transport for telemetry and model distribution. Provides QoS capabilities for differentiated service levels (safety-critical vs. operational).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operational commands and task assignments from Site Manager and Tele-operator. Worksite model updates inform task execution feedback and status.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary. Worksite model update is a core system-level function. Containment is high because the entire latency pipeline (acquisition, transmission, processing, distribution) is owned by NGHE Solution. Minimal diffusion: only external interfaces are to Vehicle (telemetry source), Network Link (transport), and consumers (operators, safety services).

Vehicle owns telemetry acquisition and initial transmission, but does not own the central worksite model service, processing, or distribution. Assigning IM to Vehicle would fragment the latency pipeline and create diffusion across Vehicle and NGHE Solution. Not recommended as primary IM.

Teleoperation is a consumer of worksite model updates, not the owner of the update pipeline. Assigning IM to Teleoperation would create high diffusion and violate separation of concerns. Not recommended.

Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert outputs derived from worksite model updates, but does not own the model update pipeline itself. Assigning IM to Safety Services would fragment the latency-critical path and create diffusion. Not recommended as primary IM.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary because it owns the end-to-end worksite model update pipeline, including telemetry ingestion, processing, persistence, and distribution. This containment minimizes diffusion and ensures unified latency management across the entire pipeline. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are consumers or sources of data, not owners of the update mechanism.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle telemetry acquisition latency is not controlled, NGHE Solution cannot meet p95 target. Mitigation: Establish clear latency budget allocation to Vehicle (e.g., ≤ 100 ms for acquisition and transmission).
Risk: If Network Link does not provide QoS support, safety-critical path may not meet latency target. Mitigation: Specify QoS requirements in Network Link interface contract.

Risk: If worksite model service processing or database write latency exceeds budget, NGHE Solution cannot meet p95 target. Mitigation: Conduct latency profiling and optimization of model fusion and persistence logic.

Risk: Coupling between safety-critical and operational paths within NGHE Solution may cause priority inversion. Mitigation: Implement explicit scheduling and resource isolation for safety-critical path.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine NGHE Solution IM boundary to explicitly define internal sub-modules for safety-critical telemetry path, operational telemetry path, model fusion service, and distribution service. Establish clear latency budgets and interface contracts for each sub-module. Define ownership of latency monitoring and p95 measurement.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid synchronous/asynchronous approach with tiered distribution) directly addresses the refined FR requirement for p95 ≤ 1.0 s worksite model update latency. The DP decouples safety-critical and operational latency requirements, enabling independent optimization. Explicit latency budgeting and path separation provide a clear solution strategy. No independence axiom violation detected: the DP is a single, coherent design concept that does not require multiple independent DPs to satisfy the FR.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: If Vehicle telemetry acquisition is not tightly integrated with NGHE Solution's telemetry ingestion, latency may exceed budget. Mitigation: Define clear interface contract and latency SLA between Vehicle and NGHE Solution.
Diffusion risk: If Network Link QoS is not guaranteed, safety-critical path may not meet latency target. Mitigation: Specify QoS requirements in Network Link interface and validate availability.

Diffusion risk: If Safety Services or Teleoperation implement their own model caching or processing, consistency and latency may diverge. Mitigation: Enforce single source of truth for worksite model within NGHE Solution; consumers access via well-defined interfaces.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution IM boundary contains the entire worksite model update pipeline (acquisition, transmission, processing, persistence, distribution). External interfaces are clearly defined: Telemetry (bidirectional with Vehicle and consumers), SafetyAlert (to Safety Services), Network Link (transport), Tasking (from operators). Containment is high; diffusion is minimal and manageable via interface contracts.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via end-to-end latency measurement across representative scenarios. Instrumentation of telemetry acquisition, transmission, processing, and distribution stages enables p95 latency calculation. Safety-critical event detection latency can be validated separately. Acceptance criteria are measurable and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns the worksite model update pipeline and is responsible for meeting p95 ≤ 1.0 s latency target. Vehicle is responsible for telemetry acquisition and transmission within allocated budget (e.g., ≤ 100 ms). Network Link is responsible for QoS support. Safety Services and Teleoperation are consumers with defined interface contracts. Ownership boundaries are explicit.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to specify latency budgets for each sub-path and define model consistency guarantees.
Establish clear interface contracts between NGHE Solution and Vehicle, Network Link, Safety Services, and Teleoperation, including latency SLAs and data format specifications.
Define latency monitoring and p95 measurement strategy within NGHE Solution (e.g., instrumentation points, data collection, analysis).
Conduct risk assessment of network QoS availability and define fallback strategies if QoS is not available.
Validate that worksite model service processing and database write latency can meet allocated budget (e.g., ≤ 500 ms for processing and persistence).

decision.justification: The refined FR, selected DP, and assigned IM are coherent and pass all gate reviews. However, the DP is not yet sufficiently detailed to guide implementation. The sub-FRs identified in the refined_fr section (FR-007.1 through FR-007.5) require further decomposition to define the internal architecture of the worksite model update pipeline. Specifically, the hybrid synchronous/asynchronous approach, latency budgeting, and tiered distribution strategy need to be decomposed into lower-level functional and design decisions. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify the internal structure of NGHE Solution and enable detailed design and implementation planning.
decision.justification_detail: The current level establishes the system-level latency requirement and selects a high-level architectural approach (hybrid synchronous/asynchronous). However, critical design decisions remain open: (1) How are safety-critical and operational telemetry paths separated and prioritized? (2) What is the internal architecture of the worksite model service (e.g., model fusion algorithm, persistence strategy)? (3) How is model distribution implemented (push vs. pull, subscription-based)? (4) What are the specific latency budgets for each sub-path and component? These questions require decomposition of the sub-FRs into lower-level functional and design decisions.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-007.1: Acquire real-time telemetry from vehicle sensors and subsystems with minimal acquisition latency (≤ 100 ms p95).
FR-007.2: Transmit telemetry data to worksite model service with bounded network latency (≤ 200 ms p95 for safety-critical, ≤ 500 ms p95 for operational).
FR-007.3: Process and fuse telemetry into worksite model state representation with bounded processing latency (≤ 300 ms p95).
FR-007.4: Persist updated worksite model state to accessible storage with bounded write latency (≤ 200 ms p95).

FR-007.5: Distribute updated worksite model to authorized consumers (operators, tele-operators, safety services) within latency budget (≤ 100 ms p95 for safety-critical alerts, ≤ 1.0 s p95 for operational updates).

assumptions: Customer can define which telemetry updates are safety-critical vs. operational.
Network infrastructure supports differentiated service levels (QoS) or can be upgraded to support QoS.
Vehicle sensors can acquire and transmit telemetry at sufficient frequency to support p95 ≤ 1.0 s model update.
Worksite model service can process and persist updates within allocated latency budget.
Safety-critical and operational latency requirements can be decoupled without compromising operational effectiveness.
Tele-operator and operator feedback loops can tolerate the allocated latency (p95 ≤ 1.0 s for operational updates).
open_risks: Risk: Sensor acquisition latency or transmission delays may exceed allocated budget, causing overall latency to exceed p95 ≤ 1.0 s target.
Risk: Network QoS may not be available or may be degraded, causing safety-critical path to miss latency target.
Risk: Worksite model service processing or database write latency may exceed allocated budget due to model complexity or database contention.
Risk: Priority inversion between safety-critical and operational paths may cause safety-critical updates to miss latency target.
Risk: Eventual consistency in operational path may cause operator confusion or safety issues if model state diverges significantly from reality.
Risk: Tele-operator feedback loop latency (command transmission + model update + feedback display) may exceed operator tolerance, impacting usability and safety.
evidence_needed: Latency profiling of Vehicle telemetry acquisition and transmission (baseline measurement).
Network latency and QoS capability assessment (baseline measurement and availability confirmation).
Worksite model service latency profiling (processing and persistence latency for representative model sizes and update frequencies).
Operator feedback loop latency measurement (end-to-end latency from command transmission to feedback display).
Safety analysis of eventual consistency in operational path (impact on hazard detection and operator response time).
Customer acceptance criteria validation (confirmation of p95 ≤ 1.0 s target and definition of safety-critical vs. operational telemetry).
Prototype or simulation of hybrid synchronous/asynchronous architecture to validate latency targets and identify bottlenecks.
